subject: Arts

sections.heading: The Quest Begins — What is Art Assessment?
sections.body: Imagine you are a young wizard on a quest. At the end of your journey, how do people know how powerful you have become? In art, assessment is how your teacher understands how much you have grown as an artist. It is not about right or wrong answers like a maths test. It looks at your creativity, how you express yourself, and how you use colours, shapes, and tools. Just like in cricket, it is not only about runs — your fielding and teamwork matter too!
keyPoints: Art assessment checks creativity, expression, and skills — not just correct answers
It helps teachers, parents, and students see how much the young artist has grown
It encourages you to explore and enjoy art, not fear tests

sections.heading: The Wizard's Tools — Methods of Assessment
sections.body: A wizard needs many spells, not just one! Similarly, art assessment uses many different methods. Your teacher might keep a portfolio — like a magic album — where all your artwork is collected over the year. There are also project-based checks where you make something and show it, and self-assessment where you yourself think about how well you did. It is like after playing a cricket match, you think — did I bat well today? Could I have done better?
keyPoints: Portfolio: a collection of all your art made during the year
Project-based: you create something and it is reviewed
Self-assessment: you reflect on your own progress, like a self-check after a game

sections.heading: Two Realms of Assessment — Formative and Summative
sections.body: In the realm of art, there are two types of assessment. Formative assessment happens throughout the year — every class, your teacher quietly watches how you work, how curious you are, and how you try new things. It is like a coach watching you practise every day at the cricket ground. Summative assessment is the big moment — like the final match! One whole day is set aside where you create something fresh, on the spot, to show all your powers together.
keyPoints: Formative: ongoing, day-by-day observations in every class
Summative: a special full-day assessment where you create something spontaneously
Both together give a complete picture of your growth

sections.heading: The Power Scale — Grades and Marks
sections.body: Every young wizard earns a level based on their mastery! In art, a 5-point scale is used. If you are just starting, you are at level 1 — Beginning. As you practise and grow, you move up to Developing, Promising, Proficient, and finally Excellent — that is level 5! It is just like levelling up in a video game. Your teacher gives you a number and a grade — from E to A — so you always know where you stand and how to improve.
keyPoints: Both scores (numbers) and teacher observations (feelings, interest, effort) are used together
The goal is to encourage you to grow, not to make you feel bad

sections.heading: The Final Dragon — Summative Assessment in Action
sections.body: The biggest challenge on the quest is the summative assessment — facing the dragon! For example, in Visual Arts, you might be asked: What does a kite mean to you? Then you design a kite in any shape, decorate it with things that make you happy, and even make it fly! In a group, you display all the kites together and talk about each other's work. This checks your creativity, teamwork, and how you express yourself — all at once, just like a full Diwali celebration where everyone brings something special!
keyPoints: Summative tasks are creative activities like designing a kite or making a puppet — not written exams
Group activities check collaboration and teamwork alongside individual creativity
You are assessed on expression, imagination, use of materials, and presentation

summary: Chapter 22 is all about how your art skills are assessed on your quest to become a true creative wizard. Unlike regular subjects, art assessment does not use simple right-or-wrong tests. Instead, it uses many magical methods — portfolios, projects, and self-reflection — to understand your unique artistic journey. There are two realms of assessment: formative, which happens every class through your teacher's observations, and summative, which is a special day where you create something fresh and exciting. Your progress is measured on a 5-point scale — from Beginning to Excellent — covering Visual Arts, Music, Dance, and Theatre. The real goal is never to scare you, but to help you discover your own powers, celebrate your creativity, and keep growing as a young artist!
